content.full_text:
P2013
89031

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

September 28, 1981

Richard Harmon, President
San Ramon Incorporation
Study Committee, Inc.
Post Office Box 54
San Ramon, CA 94583

A-81-09-069

Dear Mr. Harmon:

I am responding to your letter of September 14, 1981,
in which you ask about the requirements of the Political
Reform Act in connection with the San Ramon Incorporation
Study Committee. The Political Reform Act does not require
that your organization disband once an incorporation
question is placed on the ballot. However, the Political
Reform Act may require your organization to file statements
of receipts and expenditures under Government Code
Sections 84100 through 84217.

Under the Political Reform Act, a committee as defined
in Government Code Section 82013, must file campaign
statements showing receipts and expenditures. A group
becomes a committee for purposes of the Act when it has
received $500.00 or more in a calendar year, or has made
expenditures of $500.00 or more in a calendar year in
connection with any election campaign. A committee for a
ballot measure, including a campaign for qualification of
a measure for the ballot, is considered an election
campaign for purposes of the Act. See Government Code
Section 82031. Thus, a group such as yours is a committee
within the meaning of the Act, and must file campaign
disclosure statements, if it makes expenditures of $500.00
or more, or receives contributions of $500.00 or more,
for the purpose of qualifying a measure for the ballot,
or for campaigning for a measure once it has been placed
on the ballot.

Richard Harmon, President
September 28, 1981
Page Two

     As you describe your organization, it is still in the
preliminary study stages.  Until the time when a campaign
is begun to seek signatures to place a measure on a ballot,
the San Ramon Incorporation Study Committee will not
incur any filing obligations under the Political Reform
Act.  Once a decision is made to seek signatures for a
ballot measure, or once the measure has been placed on the
ballot and a decision is made to campaign either in favor
of or against the ballot measure, reporting obligations
will be incurred.  At that point, all receipts and all
expenditures of the committee must be reported.  In
addition, it is possible that some expenditures prior to
the time of the initiation of the signature drive for the
ballot measure or prior to the beginning of campaigning
for the ballot measure may have to be reported under the
Act.  Therefore, it would be a good idea for your committee
to keep track of all receipts and expenditures at this
point, in the event that a decision is later made that
they have to be reported.  If the committee does decide
to become involved in seeking signatures for a ballot
measure or in campaigning for or against the ballot
measure, I suggest that you contact the Technical Assistance
Division of this office at (916) 322-5662 to determine the
point at which your reporting obligations begin.

     For your convenience, I am enclosing a copy of the
Political Reform Act and a copy of our most recent campaign
reporting manual.  I also enclose a copy of our record
keeping manual, which may be useful to you in the event
that you have to file any statements under the Act.

     I hope this has answered your questions.  If you have
any further questions, please feel free to call me.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

BAM:ce

Enclosures

San Ramon Incorporation Study Committee, Inc.
P.O. BOX 54        SAN RAMON, CALIFORNIA 94583

September 14, 1981

Fair Political Practices Commission
Sacramento, CA

To whom it may concern:

Our group, the San Ramon Incorporation Study Committee, Inc. was formed in March, 1981, to study the feasibility of cityhood for the area commonly known as "San Ramon", and place the issue on a ballot if a positive benefit to incorporation were discerned.

San Ramon is the southernmost suburban area in Contra Costa County. It adjoins Danville and Dublin.(Alameda County). Both of these areas are also in the process of attempting to make cityhood a reality for themselves.

We have recently become involved in conversations that allege there is a FPPC requirement that we distribute once an incorporation question is placed on the ballot. Other arguments assert that our organization can continue to exist, foster public awareness and comment on the cityhood issues, and (most importantly) raise funds to pay for consultant studies, mailings, and other overhead of the campaign.

We would appreciate clarification of your agency's posture regarding what is and is not permissible with respect to advocating incorporation, hosting public forums, seeking funds and the time frame in which such activities are not longer allowed. At no time have we or would we become financially or organizationally involved with campaigns of individuals for city council positions.

Copies of our By-Laws and Articles of Incorporation are enclosed. Our thanks in advance for your cooperation and assistance.

Sincerely,

Richard Harmon
President
